did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (ESRGAN) for data augmentation and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including VGG16, VGG19, and a hybrid VGG19+SVM model for skin cancer classification on ISIC 2019 and 2020 datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the application of Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (ESRGAN) for data augmentation, and Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) like VGG16, VGG19, and a hybrid VGG19+SVM model for skin cancer classification. It reports specific accuracy results (e.g., 96% on ISIC 2019) on benchmark datasets (ISIC 2019 and 2020), indicating it is an original technical study rather than a review.

title: The Power of Generative AI to Augment for Enhanced Skin Cancer Classification: A Deep Learning Approach
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of skin cancer classification by employing Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (ESRGAN) for data augmentation on the ISIC 2019 and 2020 benchmark datasets. The researchers evaluated the efficacy of Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs), specifically VGG16 and VGG19, alongside a Support Vector Machine (SVM) and a hybrid VGG19+SVM model. On the ISIC 2019 dataset, the hybrid VGG19+SVM model achieved the highest accuracy of 96%, outperforming VGG16 (92%) and VGG19 (93%). For the ISIC 2020 dataset, the models achieved accuracies of 90% for VGG16, and 92% for both VGG19 and SVM. The findings underscore the potential of generative AI for augmentation and the effectiveness of CNN-based transfer learning in improving skin cancer classification accuracy.

lesion_types_studied.details: Identified as a highly lethal form of cancer and a primary focus of the study; the paper distinguishes between malignant (melanoma) and benign/non-malignant lesions.
Included in the ISIC 2019 and ISIC 2020 datasets used for classification; the study aims to differentiate malignant from benign/non-malignant lesions.

all_task_types.target: Skin lesion classes (malignant vs. benign/non-malignant) and specific diagnostic categories (e.g., melanoma, basal cell carcinoma, etc.)
all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly states the task is 'skin cancer classification' and 'classifying skin lesions'. It uses datasets ISIC 2019 (8 classes) and ISIC 2020 (2 classes: benign and malignant). The models (VGG16, VGG19, SVM, VGG19+SVM) predict discrete diagnostic labels. Accuracy metrics (92%, 93%, 96%, 90%) are reported for class prediction.

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2019
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing dermoscopic images of various skin lesions. It includes images from renowned institutions such as Hospital Clinic de Barcelona, Medical University of Vienna, Memorial Sloan Kettering Cancer Center, Melanoma Institute Australia, University of Queensland, and University of Athens Medical School. Malignant diagnoses are verified via histopathology, and benign cases via expert consensus and follow-up.

use.training: Used for training CNN models (VGG16, VGG19) and SVM after preprocessing and augmentation.
use.testing: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy reported as 92% for VGG16, 93% for VGG19, and 96% for hybrid VGG19+SVM).

datasets_used.name: ISIC 2020
datasets_used.description: A benchmark dataset from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration containing over 33,000 dermoscopic images captured using a dermoscope. It includes images of both benign and potentially cancerous skin spots collected from a diverse group of individuals.

use.training: Used for training CNN models (VGG16, VGG19) and SVM after preprocessing and augmentation.
use.testing: Used for evaluating model performance (accuracy reported as 90% for VGG16, 92% for VGG19, and 92% for SVM).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made based on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score metrics without statistical significance testing.

ISIC 2020.notes: On the non-augmented ISIC 2020 dataset, the hybrid VGG-SVM model achieved the highest accuracy (92%) and F1-score (0.92). VGG19 was the second best in precision, while SVM performed better in recall after VGG-SVM.

ISIC 2020 (with GAN-based Augmentation).notes: With GAN-based augmentation, precision, recall, and F1-score for all models increased significantly by 3% to 4%. The VGG-SVM model outperformed all others with a 96% F1-score, surpassing the state-of-the-art result of 94% reported in reference [21]. The VGG-16 model exhibited a 4% increase in F1-score compared to its non-augmented performance.

ISIC 2019.notes: On the non-augmented ISIC 2019 dataset, the hybrid VGG19+SVM model achieved the highest accuracy of 96%. VGG16 and VGG19 both revealed above 0.91 scores for precision, recall, and F1-score. VGG16 and VGG19 performed better on ISIC 2019 than on ISIC 2020.

ISIC 2019 (with GAN-based Augmentation).notes: For the augmented ISIC 2019 dataset, VGG16 and VGG19 revealed better results than on non-augmented datasets. VGG16's precision and F1-score increased by 1%, while recall remained almost the same. VGG19's precision, recall, and F1-score improved by factors of 0.01, 0.02, and 0.02 respectively.

architecture_used.main_model: Convolutional Neural Networks (CNNs) including VGG16, VGG19, and a hybrid VGG19+SVM model; Support Vector Machine (SVM) with RBF kernel.

VGG16.description: Transfer learning model used for image classification.

VGG19.description: Advanced deep neural network architecture for large-scale image classification.

SVM.description: Used for binary classification, specifically on the ISIC 2020 dataset.

VGG-SVM (Hybrid).description: Combines VGG19 for feature extraction and SVM for classification. Implemented using PyTorch.

classification_or_output_head.SVM: RBF Kernel with Sigmoid output activation for binary classification
classification_or_output_head.VGG-SVM: Non-linear SVM classifier on top of VGG feature extractor

preprocessing_techniques: Data labeling using ground truth from International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC)

Hair noise removal: conversion to grayscale, morphological kernel creation, application of MORPH_BLACKHAT to highlight dark regions, thresholding to binary format, and inpainting to fill missing/damaged regions
Resizing/Rescaling: Standardization of all images to a resolution of 224x224 pixels using a custom Python resizing algorithm to reduce size without compromising quality

Data Augmentation: Application of random rotations, cropping, and splitting using ImageDataGenerator; generation of new images using Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) and Enhanced Super-Resolution Generative Adversarial Networks (ESRGAN)

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied random rotations, cropping, and splitting to artificially expand the dataset and prevent overfitting.
Used to generate new synthetic images from existing ones to enhance data quality and quantity, address data imbalance by generating samples for classes with fewer instances, and reduce overfitting.
Used for data augmentation to improve image resolution and quality, evaluated alongside standard GANs.

conclusions: The hybrid VGG19+SVM model achieved the highest accuracy of 96% on the ISIC 2019 dataset, outperforming VGG16 (92%) and VGG19 (93%).
On the ISIC 2020 dataset, the VGG16, VGG19, and SVM models achieved accuracies of 90%, 92%, and 92%, respectively.
Data augmentation using Generative Adversarial Networks (GANs) significantly improved model performance, increasing precision, recall, and F1-scores by 3% to 4% on the ISIC 2020 dataset.
With GAN-based augmentation, the hybrid VGG-SVM model achieved a 96% F1-score on the ISIC 2020 dataset, surpassing the state-of-the-art result of 94% reported in reference [21].
The VGG16 model exhibited a 4% increase in F1-score on the augmented ISIC 2020 dataset compared to its non-augmented performance.
On the augmented ISIC 2019 dataset, VGG16 and VGG19 showed improved results compared to non-augmented datasets, with VGG16's precision and F1-score increasing by 1%.
The study underscores the potential of generative AI for data augmentation and the efficacy of CNN-based transfer learning models in improving skin cancer classification accuracy.
important_remarks: The paper is an unedited author's version accepted for publication in IEEE Access; content may change prior to final publication.
No formal statistical hypothesis testing, p-values, or confidence intervals were reported; performance comparisons rely solely on accuracy, precision, recall, and F1-score.

The study claims to surpass the state-of-the-art F1-score of 94% (reference [21]) with a 96% score on ISIC 2020, but does not provide detailed comparative analysis or ablation studies against other recent SOTA methods.

Data leakage risk exists as the paper does not explicitly describe how the ISIC 2019 and 2020 datasets were split into training, validation, and testing sets, nor does it confirm that augmentation was applied only to the training set.
The hybrid VGG19+SVM model results on ISIC 2019 report accuracy (96%) but omit precision, recall, and F1-score, making it difficult to fully assess its performance relative to other metrics.
The paper acknowledges limitations in computational complexity and lack of model interpretability, noting that future work is needed to improve clinical trustworthiness.
The study relies entirely on public benchmark datasets (ISIC 2019 and 2020) with labels verified by dermatologists, limiting external validation on independent clinical cohorts.
The preprocessing pipeline includes hair removal and resizing to 224x224 pixels, but the specific impact of these preprocessing steps on the final model performance is not isolated or quantified.
